content.text: Legacy system automation is the practice of connecting modern agents and RPA bots to systems that predate REST APIs: mainframes running COBOL or PL/I, green-screen terminals accessed via TN3270 or telnet, old Windows desktop applications built with Win32/VB6/MFC, SOAP/XML web services, FTP-based file exchanges, and relational databases exposed only through ODBC or JDBC drivers.

The central idea is that you leave the legacy system completely untouched and write a thin integration layer — a screen-scraper, a COM bridge, a file watcher, or a lightweight REST facade — that translates modern structured calls into whatever protocol the old system understands. The agent or RPA bot never needs to know it is talking to a 40-year-old batch processor.

A large fraction of critical business data — banking core ledgers, insurance policy systems, government benefit records, airline reservation engines — still lives inside IBM z/OS mainframes or AS/400 (IBM i) systems that have been running continuously since the 1970s. Replacing them takes years and hundreds of millions of dollars. Organisations cannot wait for a full rewrite before benefiting from modern AI-driven automation.

Legacy system automation bridges this gap immediately. An agent can query a COBOL batch file, fill a green-screen form, extract a number from an Excel spreadsheet via COM, or poll an FTP drop for incoming CSV files — all without touching the underlying system's code, contracts, or compliance posture.
the integration layer sits entirely outside the legacy system. No risk of destabilising certified or audited code.
a working bot can be deployed in days or weeks rather than waiting years for a full modernisation project.
the legacy system's SOX, HIPAA, or PCI audit trail is undisturbed. The bot is just another authorised user.
once you have a REST facade wrapping a legacy endpoint, you can later replace the backend without changing any downstream consumers.
bots do not need shift handovers, do not make transcription errors, and can run overnight batch cycles reliably.
pixel coordinates and field positions break when the UI is restyled, the terminal font changes, or a new release adds a row to a menu. Every UI change becomes a maintenance event.
legacy systems often have no service-account concept. Bots end up sharing a named-user credential, complicating audit trails.
a terminal-emulation bot can issue only a few transactions per second. Mainframe session limits mean you cannot simply spin up 100 parallel bots.
if the organisation never completes modernisation, the integration layer itself becomes legacy code that future teams must maintain.
a legacy system that returns an error as a specific screen label or a fixed-width field value is not obvious to parse; robust error detection requires explicit checks.
There are six integration patterns, each suited to a different legacy surface. You will often combine several in a single workflow.

a library establishes a virtual terminal session. The bot reads field positions by cursor coordinate or by named field markers in the IBM 3270 data stream, types values, presses function keys (PF3, Enter), and reads the response screen.

tools like pyautogui, pywinauto, or the RPA platform's object repository locate UI controls using Win32 HWND handles, accessibility APIs (UI Automation), or pixel matching, then send clicks and keystrokes.

applications like Excel, Word, SAP GUI, and many legacy custom tools expose a COM dispatch interface. Python's pywin32 can drive them programmatically without touching the screen at all — faster and more reliable than pixel scraping.

legacy databases (DB2, Sybase, MS Access, Informix) expose ODBC DSNs or JDBC URLs. Python's pyodbc or cx_Oracle connects directly, bypassing any UI entirely — the most reliable pattern when direct database access is permitted.

the legacy system generates or consumes batch files dropped on an FTP server or a shared network drive. The agent watches for new files, parses them (often fixed-width columns with no headers), transforms the data, and drops a reply file.
pre-REST web services expose a WSDL document. Python's zeep library parses the WSDL and generates typed client stubs automatically, so calling the service looks like a normal function call.

attrs.alt: Legacy system automation architecture: Modern Agent calls a Thin Adapter Layer which routes to mainframes, desktop apps, databases, file systems, SOAP services, and Office apps
attrs.title: Legacy System Automation — Integration Architecture

content.text: The agent (or RPA orchestrator) issues structured calls to the adapter layer. Each adapter speaks the native protocol of its target system. The legacy system itself is never modified — it simply sees another authorised session or batch job arriving over its normal channel.
The legacy system cannot be modified
— vendor contract, frozen certification, or active regulatory audit prevents code changes.
A full rewrite is 2+ years away
and business stakeholders need automation value now.
The data lives exclusively in the old system
— no shadow copy exists in a modern data warehouse, so you must go directly to the source.
— a few hundred to a few thousand transactions per day fits comfortably within terminal session limits and screen-scrape latency.
screen layouts or file formats that change rarely are safe to scrape; volatile UIs make screen scraping expensive to maintain.
Choose the lowest-friction channel available. Prefer direct database access over ODBC over screen-scraping — each step up the ladder adds fragility. Here is the decision sequence:
Check for a direct database connection (ODBC/JDBC).
If the DBA can grant read (or read/write) access, stop here — this is the most reliable option.
Check for a SOAP/XML API or FTP drop.
Many mainframe shops added a thin SOAP layer years ago. Parse the WSDL with zeep and use the generated client.
Check for COM / OLE (Windows desktop apps).
Open the object model browser (oleview.exe) to enumerate the application's dispatch interface. If it has one, use pywin32 — much faster than screen scraping.
Fall back to terminal emulation (TN3270) or GUI screen scraping
only when none of the above are available. Document every field position and menu path in a specification file so that UI changes are localised to one place.
Wrap the adapter in a thin REST service
(FastAPI or Flask) so the agent treats legacy like any modern API. This decouples the integration detail from the orchestration logic.
SSH / Telnet terminal automation with Paramiko
Many legacy systems (AS/400, HP-UX batch runners, old Unix systems) are accessible via SSH. Paramiko opens an interactive shell channel, sends commands, and reads back the output.

import paramiko
import time

def run_cobol_batch_job(
    host: str,
    username: str,
    password: str,
    job_name: str,
) -> str:
    """
    SSH into a legacy Unix host, submit a COBOL batch job by running
    the compiled program, and return its stdout output.
    """
    client = paramiko.SSHClient()
    client.set_missing_host_key_policy(paramiko.AutoAddPolicy())
    client.connect(host, username=username, password=password, timeout=30)

    # Run the job and capture output
    stdin, stdout, stderr = client.exec_command(f"/opt/jobs/{job_name}")
    stdin.close()

    # Block until the command finishes
    exit_code = stdout.channel.recv_exit_status()
    output = stdout.read().decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
    error = stderr.read().decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
    client.close()

    if exit_code != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"Job {job_name!r} failed (exit {exit_code}):\n{error}"
        )
    return output


def parse_fixed_width_report(raw: str, column_specs: list[tuple[int, int, str]]) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Parse a fixed-width COBOL report into a list of dicts.

    column_specs: list of (start, end, field_name) — 0-indexed, end exclusive.
    Example:
        [(0, 9, 'account_id'), (9, 29, 'name'), (29, 38, 'balance')]
    """
    rows = []
    for line in raw.splitlines():
        if not line.strip():
            continue
        row = {}
        for start, end, name in column_specs:
            row[name] = line[start:end].strip()
        rows.append(row)
    return rows


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    raw_output = run_cobol_batch_job(
        host="mainframe.corp.internal",
        username="svcbot01",
        password="s3cr3t",
        job_name="RPT_ACCT_BALANCE",
    )

    specs = [(0, 10, "account_id"), (10, 40, "holder_name"), (40, 50, "balance")]
    records = parse_fixed_width_report(raw_output, specs)
    for rec in records[:5]:
        print(rec)
COM / OLE automation for Excel and legacy apps with pywin32

pywin32 exposes the Windows COM dispatch interface. Any application that registers a COM server (Excel, Word, SAP GUI for Windows, many legacy ERP clients) can be driven with this approach — no screen scraping, no pixel coordinates.

import win32com.client
import pythoncom

def read_legacy_excel_report(path: str, sheet_name: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Open a legacy Excel .xls file (pre-2007 format) via COM,
    read the named sheet, and return rows as a list of dicts.
    COM must be initialised if called from a non-main thread.
    """
    pythoncom.CoInitialize()  # required when calling from a worker thread
    try:
        xl = win32com.client.Dispatch("Excel.Application")
        xl.Visible = False          # run headless
        xl.DisplayAlerts = False

        wb = xl.Workbooks.Open(path)
        ws = wb.Sheets(sheet_name)

        # Read used range
        used = ws.UsedRange
        headers = [used.Cells(1, c).Value for c in range(1, used.Columns.Count + 1)]
        rows = []
        for r in range(2, used.Rows.Count + 1):
            row = {}
            for c, header in enumerate(headers, start=1):
                row[header] = used.Cells(r, c).Value
            rows.append(row)

        wb.Close(SaveChanges=False)
        xl.Quit()
        return rows
    finally:
        pythoncom.CoUninitialize()


def drive_legacy_erp_com(prog_id: str, transaction_code: str, payload: dict) -> str:
    """
    Drive a legacy ERP application that exposes a COM dispatch interface.
    prog_id is the ProgID registered in the Windows registry, e.g.
    'SAPGuiAuto' for SAP GUI scripting or 'MyERP.Application'.
    """
    app = win32com.client.GetActiveObject(prog_id)  # attach to running instance
    session = app.GetSession(0)
    session.StartTransaction(transaction_code)

    for field_id, value in payload.items():
        session.FindById(field_id).Text = str(value)

    session.FindById("wnd[0]/tbar[0]/btn[0]").Press()  # Enter / OK
    status = session.FindById("wnd[0]/sbar").Text
    return status

import pyodbc
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def legacy_db_connection(dsn: str, uid: str, pwd: str):
    """
    Context manager for a legacy ODBC connection (DB2, Sybase, Informix, etc.).
    Commits on clean exit, rolls back on error.
    """
    conn = pyodbc.connect(f"DSN={dsn};UID={uid};PWD={pwd}", autocommit=False)
    try:
        yield conn
        conn.commit()
    except Exception:
        conn.rollback()
        raise
    finally:
        conn.close()


def fetch_policy_records(dsn: str, uid: str, pwd: str, min_balance: float) -> list[dict]:
    with legacy_db_connection(dsn, uid, pwd) as conn:
        cursor = conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute(
            """
            SELECT POLICY_ID, HOLDER_NAME, PREMIUM, EXPIRY_DATE
            FROM POLICY_MASTER
            WHERE PREMIUM > ?
            ORDER BY EXPIRY_DATE
            """,
            (min_balance,),
        )
        columns = [desc[0] for desc in cursor.description]
        return [dict(zip(columns, row)) for row in cursor.fetchall()]
REST adapter wrapping a legacy screen-scraper
The facade pattern wraps the fragile screen-scraper in a FastAPI service. Agents call a clean REST API; only the adapter knows about terminal coordinates or file formats.

"""legacy_adapter.py — REST facade over a TN3270 / SSH legacy system."""
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
import paramiko

app = FastAPI(title="Legacy System Adapter", version="1.0")

class AccountQuery(BaseModel):
    account_id: str

class AccountBalance(BaseModel):
    account_id: str
    holder_name: str
    balance: float
    currency: str

# Connection pool (simplified — use a proper pool in production)
_SSH_HOST = "mainframe.corp.internal"
_SSH_USER = "svcbot01"
_SSH_KEY  = "/etc/bot/id_rsa"

def _query_balance_via_ssh(account_id: str) -> dict:
    client = paramiko.SSHClient()
    client.set_missing_host_key_policy(paramiko.RejectPolicy())  # no auto-trust in prod
    client.connect(_SSH_HOST, username=_SSH_USER, key_filename=_SSH_KEY)
    _, stdout, stderr = client.exec_command(f"QUERY_BALANCE {account_id}")
    rc = stdout.channel.recv_exit_status()
    out = stdout.read().decode()
    client.close()
    if rc != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(stderr.read().decode())
    # Parse fixed-width response: columns 0-9 id, 10-39 name, 40-50 balance, 51-53 currency
    return {
        "account_id":  out[0:10].strip(),
        "holder_name": out[10:40].strip(),
        "balance":     float(out[40:50].strip()),
        "currency":    out[51:54].strip(),
    }

@app.post("/accounts/balance", response_model=AccountBalance)
def get_balance(query: AccountQuery):
    try:
        data = _query_balance_via_ssh(query.account_id)
    except Exception as exc:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=502, detail=str(exc))
    return AccountBalance(**data)

# Run: uvicorn legacy_adapter:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080

A large European insurance company needed to process 3,000 daily policy renewals that required reading from an IBM AS/400 (IBM i) system over TN5250, running a rating calculation in a separate Win32 actuarial desktop tool, and writing results back as a fixed-width file dropped on an FTP server for batch import.

The team built three adapters: a Paramiko-based SSH connector that queried the AS/400 via a compiled RPGLE query program, a pywin32 COM client that drove the actuarial tool's undocumented COM server (discovered via oleview.exe), and a Python ftplib uploader that deposited the output file. A UiPath orchestrator called all three adapters in sequence, looping over a CSV of account IDs produced by an overnight COBOL batch job. The entire automation was live in six weeks. The AS/400 and the actuarial tool were not touched — they simply saw authorised users submitting normal transactions.
Hard-coding pixel coordinates instead of control identifiers:

Win32 UI Automation (accessible via pywinauto) uses stable control names and AutomationIds, not screen positions. Use these when available; fall back to pixels only for applications with no accessibility tree.
mainframes show a "X SYSTEM" or "NOT IN SERVICE" message during maintenance windows. A bot that does not recognise these states silently writes garbage data.
Issuing too many concurrent terminal sessions:
mainframes licence by concurrent session count. Running 20 bots in parallel may exceed the licence and lock out human users.
Skipping CoInitialize in multi-threaded COM use:
COM requires each thread that uses it to call
. Forgetting this in a thread pool causes cryptic RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD errors.
legacy systems often lack OAuth or API keys. Use a secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) and fetch credentials at runtime.
Parsing fixed-width files by splitting on whitespace:
names and addresses often contain embedded spaces. Always slice by absolute column offsets as defined in the record layout specification.
Externalise all field positions and menu paths
into a configuration file (YAML or JSON). When the terminal UI changes, update config — not code.
Add an explicit "screen check" before every action:
after navigating to a screen, verify a known stable label (e.g. "ACCOUNT INQUIRY" at row 1, col 30) before reading fields. Abort with a clear error rather than parsing the wrong screen silently.
Prefer service accounts over shared human credentials
— even if the legacy system calls them "user accounts". Separate credentials keep the audit log clean.
Rate-limit terminal sessions to stay under licence caps.
Use a semaphore or connection pool with a hard maximum matching the purchased session count.
Wrap every adapter in a REST facade from day one.
This isolates the messy legacy protocol from your agent code and creates an obvious seam to replace when the system is eventually modernised.
Log every transaction with a timestamp, session ID, and the raw screen or file content
— not just the parsed result. When a bug surfaces, this log is the only way to reconstruct what the system actually returned.
Legacy system automation has hard ceilings that do not exist in modern microservices. Understanding them prevents capacity mistakes.

a single TN3270 session can submit roughly 2–5 transactions per second (screen paint + network round trip). With 10 licensed concurrent sessions you reach roughly 20–50 tps — adequate for overnight batch, not for real-time high-volume.

most legacy COM servers are in-process STA servers. You can run multiple COM client processes, but within a single process COM calls to an STA object are serialised. Parallelism requires separate processes, not threads.

direct database access via pyodbc can sustain thousands of queries per second limited only by the database engine, not by the integration layer. When ODBC is available it is always preferable to screen-scraping for throughput.
File-based integration scales by file size, not row count:
parsing a 2 GB fixed-width file in Python using line-by-line iteration is memory-efficient. Use
with explicit record-length slicing rather than
to avoid loading the whole file into memory.

SSH and ODBC connections have non-trivial setup latency (TLS + auth negotiation). For high-throughput scenarios, maintain a pool of pre-authenticated connections rather than opening a new one per request.
Interactive shell channels, SCP/SFTP file transfer
Wraps x3270 / s3270 emulator; field-level reads
Excel, Word, SAP GUI scripting, legacy ERP
Finds controls by title/class; more stable than pixel scraping
Last resort for apps with no COM or accessibility API
DB2, Sybase, Informix, MS Access, SQL Server
Auto-generates typed client from WSDL; MTOM attachment support
Enterprise RPA platform with built-in TN3270, SAP, Excel connectors
Strong mainframe / green-screen toolkit; central control room
IQ Bot for unstructured document extraction from legacy reports
Legacy systems with existing SOAP layer
Long-term; not an option when speed is critical
Legacy systems often predate modern security practices. Integrating them requires deliberate hardening at the adapter layer.
Reject unknown host keys in Paramiko:
. Pre-populate known_hosts from the bastion server's fingerprint.
store the private key in a secrets manager, not on disk. Rotate keys on a schedule.
to your DSN connection string. Some older ODBC drivers default to plaintext.
Scope FTP / SFTP access by directory:
use chroot jails or SFTP subsystem path restrictions so the bot can only read its designated drop folder, not traverse the entire file system.
Sanitise inputs before passing to terminal sessions:
a mainframe user typing PF3 can navigate backwards; a bot passing untrusted data that maps to a function key sequence could traverse menus it should not see.
Circuit-breaker pattern for the REST facade:
if the legacy system is in maintenance mode, surface a 503 immediately rather than queuing 10,000 retries that thrash the terminal session pool.
if the bot crashes mid-transaction, it may retry a write. Implement idempotency keys or query-before-write checks to avoid creating duplicate records in the legacy system.
The core principle is non-invasive integration:

leave the legacy system untouched and build a thin adapter layer that translates modern structured calls into whatever protocol the old system understands — TN3270, SSH command, COM dispatch, ODBC SQL, FTP file, or SOAP XML.
Choose the most structured channel available.
Prefer ODBC/JDBC > SOAP > COM > terminal emulation > pixel scraping. Each step toward pixel scraping adds fragility and maintenance cost.
Wrap every adapter in a REST facade.
FastAPI sitting in front of the adapter decouples the agent from legacy protocol details and creates the natural seam for future replacement.
Screen-scraper positions and field specs belong in config, not code.
A UI change should require updating a YAML file, not a pull request with unit tests.
Use semaphores or connection pools to cap concurrency at the purchased terminal-session count. Overrunning the licence blocks human users.
Log raw responses, not just parsed results.
When a bug surfaces six months later, the raw screen or file content is the only way to diagnose what the legacy system actually returned.
Harden the adapter, not the legacy system.

Reject unknown SSH host keys, encrypt ODBC connections, store credentials in a secrets manager, and add circuit breakers at the facade layer. The legacy system cannot be patched — the adapter must compensate.
